title: Psalms 48
subtitle: Great Is the LORD in the City of Our God

sections.header: Verses 1–8 — “Mount Zion, Beautiful in Its Loftiness”

heb.paragraph: "Great is the LORD, and most worthy of praise, in the city of our God, his holy mountain. Beautiful in its loftiness, the joy of the whole earth — Mount Zion (Ṣiyyōn)" (vv.1–2). A Zion hymn. The city is described as the joy of the whole earth, the city of the Great King. Kings assembled against it and were terrified (vv.4–6). God establishes it forever (v.8).

notes.note: "Beautiful in its loftiness, the joy of the whole earth — Mount Zion." The earthly Zion is a shadow of the heavenly one (Heb 12:22). The beauty is both physical and theological.
"Kings joined forces, but when they saw it, they were astounded; they fled in terror." Zion’s enemies are overwhelmed by God’s presence within it. The city is invincible because God is inside.

refs.note: Jesus calls Jerusalem 'the city of the Great King,' directly quoting this psalm.
Coming to Mount Zion, the city of the living God, reinterprets the psalm's Zion theology.
The holy city coming down from heaven, brilliant with God's glory, fulfills Mount Zion imagery.

hist.context: Psalm 48 is a Song of Zion celebrating Jerusalem's beauty and impregnability. 'Mount Zion, the utmost heights of Zaphon' (v.2) ironically applies the name of the Canaanite divine mountain (Baal's abode) to YHWH's dwelling. The psalm may commemorate a specific military deliverance, perhaps the same Assyrian withdrawal mentioned in Isaiah 36-37.

sections.header: Verses 9–14 — “This God Is Our God for Ever and Ever”

heb.paragraph: "This God is our God for ever and ever (ʿōlām); he will be our guide even to the end" (v.14). The psalm’s closing confession: permanent possession of a permanent God. Walk around Zion, count her towers, note her ramparts (vv.12–13). Tell the next generation: this God — this specific God, the one of Zion — is ours forever.

notes.note: "Within your temple, O God, we meditate on your unfailing love. Like your name, O God, your praise reaches to the ends of the earth." Temple meditation produces worldwide testimony.

"Walk about Zion, count her towers. Note well her ramparts. Tell it to the next generation: this God is our God forever." Physical observation leads to theological confession leads to intergenerational transmission.

refs.note: Walking around the new Jerusalem to count its gates and towers fulfills the psalm's pilgrimage command.
The joy of going to the house of the LORD parallels this psalm's processional celebration.
The Jerusalem above as our mother reinterprets the earthly Zion the psalm celebrates.

hist.context: The command to walk about Zion, count its towers, and consider its ramparts (vv.12-13) suggests a liturgical procession. Pilgrims were to observe Jerusalem's defenses as evidence of divine protection, then 'tell the next generation' — the psalm functions as pedagogical liturgy. 'This God is our God forever' declares covenant permanence.

ppl.name: God (Yahweh)
ppl.role: Zion's true defense who shatters enemy coalitions (vv.1-3,8,14)
ppl.text: God is 'great and most worthy of praise, in the city of our God' (v.1). Mount Zion is 'beautiful in its loftiness, the joy of the whole earth' (v.2) — a bold claim for a modest hill, but the psalm argues that God's presence, not geography, creates significance. When kings allied against Jerusalem, 'they saw her and were astounded; they fled in terror' (vv.5-6). God's defense of Zion becomes proof of his covenant faithfulness 'to the ends of the earth' (v.10).

ppl.name: The Sons of Korah
ppl.text: This Zion song invites worshipers to 'walk about Zion, go around her, count her towers, consider well her ramparts' (vv.12-13) — a liturgical procession that doubles as a theology lesson. The city's intact walls prove God's protective faithfulness. The psalm instructs the community to 'tell of it to the next generation' (v.13), making architectural observation a vehicle for intergenerational faith transmission.

ppl.name: The Allied Kings
ppl.role: Enemy coalition that crumbles before God's city (vv.4-7)
ppl.text: The 'kings joined forces and advanced together' (v.4) against Zion, but upon seeing it 'they were astounded… they fled in terror… trembling seized them there, pain like that of a woman in labor' (vv.5-6). The historical referent may be Sennacherib's failed siege (2 Kgs 18-19) or a composite of Jerusalem's deliverances. The simile of east-wind-shattered ships (v.7, 'ships of Tarshish') suggests God wields nature itself as a military weapon against earthly superpowers.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVthey saw her and were astounded; they fled in terror.ESVAs soon as they saw it, they were astounded; they were in panic; they took to flight.NIVYou destroyed them like ships of Tarshish shattered by an east wind.ESVBy the east wind you shattered the ships of Tarshish.

rec.text: ""Beautiful in its loftiness, the joy of the whole earth — Mount Zion." The earthly Zion is a shadow of the heavenly one (Heb 12:22). The beauty is both physical and theological."

rows.label: Forever Our God — 48:9–14
rows.text: "Walk around Zion, count her towers." Tell the next generation. "Our God forever."

hebtext.note: "Great is the LORD, and most worthy of praise, in the city of our God, his holy mountain. Beautiful in its loftiness, the joy of the whole earth — Mount Zion (Ṣiyyōn)" (vv.1–2). A Zion hymn. The city is described as the joy of the whole earth, the city of the Great King. Kings assembled against it and were terrified (vv.4–6). God establishes it forever (v.8).

"This God is our God for ever and ever (ʿōlām); he will be our guide even to the end" (v.14). The psalm’s closing confession: permanent possession of a permanent God. Walk around Zion, count her towers, note her ramparts (vv.12–13). Tell the next generation: this God — this specific God, the one of Zion — is ours forever.

themes.note: Worship and Sovereignty peak: Zion hymn celebrates God’s presence in the city. Holiness: the holy mountain. Covenant: this God is OURS forever.
